title: Per-contract Buffered Commitment Allocation Sheet Q4 2029
preview:
Purpose:
This working sheet captures per-contract reserved capacity allocations with a buffered commit model. The goal is to map contractual GPU-hour commitments into operational slots across regions and GPU classes, include a configurable buffer for burst capacity, and maintain utilization targets and rollover rule...
text:
Purpose:
This working sheet captures per-contract reserved capacity allocations with a buffered commit model. The goal is to map contractual GPU-hour commitments into operational slots across regions and GPU classes, include a configurable buffer for burst capacity, and maintain utilization targets and rollover rules per customer.

Summary of approach:
- Convert monetary/commitment terms into a canonical unit: GPU-slot-hours (GSH) where 1 GSH = 1 GPU of the specified class for 1 hour. Different GPU classes (A4, V100, H100-80G) have conversion factors to normalize horsepower (see GPU_CLASS_FACTOR).
- Allocate base entitlement per contract by region according to geographic constraints and contract clauses.
- Reserve a buffer (default 18% of base GSH) in each region for short-term burst. Buffer is shared across contracts in fairness pool unless contract forbids pooling.
- Track soft-util...

text:
Opportunistic Fleet Fill — playbook scratch notes (week Sep 2–8, 2029)

Summary:
- Goal: safely increase usable capacity by filling a controlled fraction of the fleet with opportunistic (preemptible/spot) nodes during low-demand windows while preserving latency SLOs and minimizing evictions impact on customer traffic.
- This doc is a working scratchpad: experiment results, quick heuristics, proposed runbook steps, and unanswered questions. Not final.

What's changed this week:
- Ran two short A/B windows in us-west and ap-southeast (local nights): 02:00–05:30 local.
 - us-west: opportunistic fill = 8% of pool for 3.5h, median latency unchanged, P95 +18ms.
 - ap-southeast: opportunistic fill = 12% of pool for 3h, saw 2.4% eviction churn and transient retry spike on platform routes with no user-visible errors (retry layer absorbed most).
- Quick win: small scheduler tweak reduced KV-cac...

Purpose:
This note is a concise customer-facing briefing and post-incident summary we will publish on the status page and email to impacted tenants. It includes a short timeline, user-visible impact, high level root cause, immediate mitigations we implemented, suggested self-serve actions for customers, and next steps (permanent fixes + compensations).

Executive summary (for customers)
- Incident window: 2029-08-29 21:17 UTC to 2029-08-30 03:42 UTC (partial degradation; full mitigation completed).
- Impact: Intermittent truncated streaming responses and sporadic stale completions for ~0.6% of requests in affected regions (us-west and eu-west). A subset of streaming sessions to /v1/streams intermittently returned truncated JSON or `<STREAM-END>` markers early.
- User impact: Affected applications may have seen malformed structured outputs (function-calls, JSON blobs) and occasional ou...

Purpose:
Quick, messy synthesis of field feedback from Aug–Sep 2028 cadence calls, customer interviews, and in-product reports. Goal: surface low-friction patches we can prototype in Q4 + a compact backlog of medium-impact asks to validate with product. Not an exhaustive list — this is a working ledger (notes to self).

Executive snapshot (2 lines):
- High-frequency asks cluster around three usable pain points: (1) unpredictable tail latency for interactive chat at >1k tokens, (2) inability to pin model variant fallbacks per route, and (3) embedding freshness / stale-updates for streaming pipelines.
- Several customers ask for small UX/observability changes that materially reduce ops load (token-level cost attribution on error traces; per-request trace links in alert emails). Those are quick wins.

Signal clusters (raw -> theme):

text:
Context:
This is a working lab note for a small-form benchmark we started to characterize microprofile latency-cost behavior under 'pingcurve' shaped request patterns. The aim is to produce a short collateral-style snippet (text + ascii charts) we can paste into Console tooltips and the team deck. NOT a polished report — contains raw numbers, hypotheses, and TODOs.

Motivation:
- Many of our SLO conversations use steady-state throughput numbers; real clients have microbursts and interleaved low-rate background traffic.
- We want a compact artifact illustrating how p50/p95/p99 and amortized cost per token move across three dimensions: request cadence (steady vs pingcurve), model variant (fp16 vs int4-quant), and batching strategy (smallbatch vs multiplex).

Definition: 'pingcurve' workload
- A synthetic waveform: base idle rate 0.2 qps, then a 1s 'ping' spike to 25 qps, then decay back...

Sprint purpose and framing
------------------------
Quick capture from this week: stitching together three parallel threads — compacting KV-cache storage, onboarding the 4-bit variant for internal evals, and stabilizing microbatch behavior under burst load. Goal this sprint: get a reproducible eval pipeline that reports both MRR/EM-style quality metrics and token-level performance + p95 latency for v0 and v4 (4-bit) variants so we can make a go/no-go for a limited canary.

Attendees (sync Thu 2028-05-16, 10:00 PT)
- Priya Natarajan (lead applied ML)
- Ethan Morgan (perf infra)
- Sofia Alvarez (evals)
- Liu Wei (runtime / kv-cache)
- Ravi Patel (solutions eng)
- Optional FYI: Jenna K. (product) dropped a quick note about customer rollout constraints

High-level decisions made
- Proceed with a small canary (10% traffic slice) for the 4-bit model once the eval pipeline hits parity thresh...

title: Precision cost curves: multi-region quantization & batching tradeoff (Q2 2028)
preview:
Purpose:
This working sheet captures measured throughput, estimated cost, and quality delta for a set of candidate quantization + batching operating points across three target regions. The goal is to identify Pareto-efficient operating points that meet regional SLA latency targets while minimizing monthly GPU spend ...
text:
Purpose:
This working sheet captures measured throughput, estimated cost, and quality delta for a set of candidate quantization + batching operating points across three target regions. The goal is to identify Pareto-efficient operating points that meet regional SLA latency targets while minimizing monthly GPU spend for Q2 2028 commitments.

How to read this sheet:
- Quant levels tested: FP16 (baseline), INT8 (calibrated), 4-bit (GPTQ-like), 3-bit (experimental, high degradation).
- Batching policies: fixed-batch-N, adaptive-batching (autosize by latency target), prefetched-KV (KV cache hit assumed), and background-emb (background embedding pool with relaxed latency).
- Quality delta: measured as relative eval score drop on a small internal prompt-set (range 0% = no degradation, larger negative values = worse). We provide both semantic embedding cosine drop and LLM generation regressio...

preview:

Recommendation below is a staged canary (5% -> 25%) with tight mon...
text:
Summary:

Quick take: v2.4 (alpha) shows a modest overall win on our Variant Garden workload (+4.1% human-pref), but introduces a small regression on the invoice/format-preservation cohort and has a 12% higher median latency in the 2048-token regime. Recommendation below is a staged canary (5% -> 25%) with tight monitoring and a rollback trigger on >3% user-visible regression for two consecutive days.

Background / motivation:
- This was an onboarding evaluation for candidate model v2.4 (training/config changes: refactored attention block, updated tokenizer handling for emojis, mixed-precision kernel changes).
- Goal: determine whether v2.4 should be promoted from internal candidate to the hosted catalog variant replacing v2.3.
- Workload mix: Variant Garden (multi-domain promptset constructed by SE/Prod for broad coverage). Includes: chat assistant queries, code generation snippets, ...

title: Tiling burst amortization playbook — latency vs cost sketches
preview:
Purpose and scope:

This is a working playbook sketch for how platform should reason about “tiling burst amortization” — a phrasing we’re using for the pattern where short-duration high-concurrency spikes (bursts) are absorbed by dividing work into fixed-tiling slots (pre-warmed/reserved tiling) versus fully opportu...
text:
Purpose and scope:

This is a working playbook sketch for how platform should reason about “tiling burst amortization” — a phrasing we’re using for the pattern where short-duration high-concurrency spikes (bursts) are absorbed by dividing work into fixed-tiling slots (pre-warmed/reserved tiling) versus fully opportunistic continuous batching. The goal: give product/serving owners a compact set of tradeoffs, simple numeric heuristics, and example curves so PMs and SREs can pick a configuration with predictable SLO/cost tradeoffs.

Background / quick reminder:
- Bursty traffic (50–500 reqs/s for O(10s) windows) is common for interactive features (search, chat start).
- Two orthogonal levers we tune: slot-tiling (pre-reserving N tiling slots per pod with warm kernels) and continuous opportunistic batching (windowed gather on arrival).
- Key costs: steady-state reserved GPU minutes, amort...

title: Keepalive suppression and NAT reflexive race — investigation notes
preview:
Summary:

This is a working investigation into a recurring pattern we observed in three customer Private deployments (region-eu1, region-us-west2, customer-beta-cluster) where control-plane TCP sessions from control-plane proxies to customer firewalls/edge boxes were silently dropping after ~90s of idle time despite...
text:
Summary:

This is a working investigation into a recurring pattern we observed in three customer Private deployments (region-eu1, region-us-west2, customer-beta-cluster) where control-plane TCP sessions from control-plane proxies to customer firewalls/edge boxes were silently dropping after ~90s of idle time despite keepalives configured. Symptoms were asymmetric: the customer-side reported active sessions and responded to probes, but our control-plane reported connection resets/timeouts followed by immediate re-establishment attempts (which sometimes failed due to NAT translation race).

Why this doc exists (notes to self):
- capture timeline + packet-level observations in one place
- draft mitigations and runbook flips for oncall
- propose telemetry additions so next oncall can root faster

High-level hypothesis (short):
NAT device or inline firewall is doing aggressive connection p...

Purpose:
This note captures the Applied ML weekly standups and research updates for the quantization + model onboarding track. Focus this week: finish initial INT8/FP16 onboarding for two community models (Llama-3-13B variant + open-13B-mix), run cross-evals, and validate KV-cache interaction with our continuous batching path.

High-level summary (Nov 2–6):
- INT8 baseline builds completed for both targets; runtime artifacts pushed to staging nodes.
- Early eval: token-level perplexity +0.5–1.2% vs FP16 for conversational prompts; no major hallucination patterns detected in the sampled slices.
- Throughput: batching + KV-cache enabled increased steady-state tokens/sec by ~2.4x on A100-40G for short-chat workloads.
- Primary blocker: intermittent OOM when running sequence-length=4096 synthetic stress tests in combination with prefill caching (suspect KV-store eviction logic).
- Decisio...

title: capacity-scan-hosted-api-peak-supplycurve-by-az-gpu-class-2027q2
preview:
Summary:
This sheet defines a supply-curve approach to convert projected hosted API peak demand (tokens/sec and QPS) into an actionable GPU provisioning plan by availability zone (AZ) and GPU class. Aim: ensure 99th-percentile latency SLOs for conversational + completion workloads while minimizing over-provisioning ...
text:
Summary:
This sheet defines a supply-curve approach to convert projected hosted API peak demand (tokens/sec and QPS) into an actionable GPU provisioning plan by availability zone (AZ) and GPU class. Aim: ensure 99th-percentile latency SLOs for conversational + completion workloads while minimizing over-provisioning across regions.

Key differences vs prior playbooks:
- Uses an explicit supply-curve (tokens/sec -> GPU instances) per AZ and per GPU family (T4, A100, H100) instead of global headroom buckets.
- Incorporates a microburst amplification factor (MAF) and prefix-caching reuse to model short-window peaks (1m, 5m, 15m).
- Explicit fallback mapping to cheaper GPU class under saturated conditions (A100 -> T4 fallback for low-context workloads).

Assumptions (conservative baseline):
- Baseline mix: 60% short-chat (avg 80 tokens/request), 30% assistant-long (avg 600 tokens/request),...

title: Model Curation Audit Runbook — Jun 2026
preview:
Purpose:
This runbook documents a repeatable protocol for making onboarding decisions when multiple candidate model versions show near-parity across core metrics. It focuses on: (1) consistent prompt-set construction, (2) deterministic tie-breaking heuristics, (3) spot-check sampling and audit buckets, and (4) a sho...
text:
Purpose:
This runbook documents a repeatable protocol for making onboarding decisions when multiple candidate model versions show near-parity across core metrics. It focuses on: (1) consistent prompt-set construction, (2) deterministic tie-breaking heuristics, (3) spot-check sampling and audit buckets, and (4) a short human-review escalation path. Goal: reduce flip-flopping between close models and provide traceable rationale for choosing a primary serving version or opting for staged rollouts.

Context and motivation:
We regularly see small deltas between candidate checkpoints (quantized vs fp16 variants, different tokenizers, small loRA adapter differences). Standard leaderboard averages often hide distributional failures and rare-but-important regressions. This runbook operationalizes an approval flow that balances automated metrics with low-cost human validation.

Datasets and pro...

Purpose

This is a working synthesis of customer feedback (May 2026 -> early Jun 2026) focused on actionable asks for engineering/product handoff. Goal: present triaged flags, quick wins, and a mini-roadmap (0–3 months, 3–6 months) to align CS, product-hosted-api, and eng-serving-runtime on near-term deliveries and trade-offs.

Data sources & methodology
- Inputs: 28 support tickets, 12 CSM post-demo notes, 6 enterprise onboarding calls, two NPS follow-ups, and ad-hoc Slack threads (see linked_artifacts).
- Tagging: each ask tagged by product area (hosted-api, dedicated, private, console, optimize) and by signal strength (High/Medium/Low) based on recurrence and customer revenue impact.
- Triage: CS did first-pass grouping; product team applied effort estimate (S/M/L) and preliminary ROI ranking.

Purpose: weekly standing sync for on-prem / VPC installs in progress. Share current customer blockers, triage escalations, and assign next actions. This doc is intentionally loose — capture raw standup notes, test output snippets, TODOs and follow-ups.

title: Buffer Sizing Sensitivity Study — Autoscaler Trigger Experiments
preview:
Overview:
Purpose: quantify sensitivity of latency, cold-start events, and unit cost to different 'buffer' sizing strategies and min/max node bounds across regions. This is a lightweight spreadsheet-style experiment log and the distilled numbers we discussed in the infra sync (4/20).

text:
Overview:
Purpose: quantify sensitivity of latency, cold-start events, and unit cost to different 'buffer' sizing strategies and min/max node bounds across regions. This is a lightweight spreadsheet-style experiment log and the distilled numbers we discussed in the infra sync (4/20).

Hypotheses:
- Small buffer (1-2 spare GPUs) reduces cost but may increase cold-starts during bursty traffic, hurting p95 latency.
- Larger buffer (3-5) stabilizes p95 at the expense of steady-state cost.
- Region-level traffic patterns (APAC burstiness vs NA steady) change the optimal min setting.

Experiment matrix (each row = single experiment run, 48h window, synthetic workload with 30m peak bursts every 3h):

Columns: experiment_id | region | min_gpus | max_gpus | trigger_up_util% | trigger_down_util% | buffer_size_spare_gpus | observed_cold_starts_total | p95_latency_ms | avg_util_peak% | cost_delta...

text:
Purpose:
Quick, ad-hoc table to surface who we loop for short handbacks and a lightweight $/effort sample per owner. Intended for product, SRE, and SEs to decide temporary coverage vs. escalations for next 7 days. Not an SLA—just a working econ sketch.

Notes about approach:
- We bucket owners by likely effort (low/med/high) for a one-week coverage window.
- Dollar sampler is back-of-envelope: assume blended fully-burdened hourly rate = $85/hr for contributor time, 40% probability-of-involvement for med, 80% for high, 20% for low.
- This spreadsheet is intentionally compact — use as tie-breaker when assigning rapid handbacks.

Roster table (rows are manual entries from quick calls):

title: Microseq regression ledger — KV fragmentation & promptset diffs
preview: Short summary: After toggling the new paged-KV allocator (PR 8a7f3d1c) and enabling the continuous-batching scheduler on canary, we observed a small but consistent regression on a subset of micro-sequence prompt sets. The regression manifests as token-level latency spikes at sequence boundaries and a 1.8% increase i...
text: Short summary: After toggling the new paged-KV allocator (PR 8a7f3d1c) and enabling the continuous-batching scheduler on canary, we observed a small but consistent regression on a subset of micro-sequence prompt sets. The regression manifests as token-level latency spikes at sequence boundaries and a 1.8% increase in hallucination triggers on targeted QA prompts. Behavioral diffs are concentrated on prompts with repeated entity co-reference and short context windows (<96 tokens). This ledger captures the prompt-level diffs, reproduction notes, metric snapshots, and proposed mitigations for the release team.

text:
Purpose:
This doc captures an experimental, orchestrated approach to SDK onramp—mixing automated checks, short mentor sessions, and telemetry gates to accelerate integrator success while catching early regressions. It is a working chronicle: notes from three pilots, a proposed recipe (playbook), and a pocket quickstart for field engineers and solutions reps.

Principles (short):
- Teach by doing: 20–30 minute guided walkthroughs where the user ships a mini-integration.
- Telemetry-first: lightweight events define success signals and gating for rollouts.
- Fast recovery: provide tiny fallbacks and clear rollback paths in the first commit.
- Low friction: aim for zero config for the first demo; expose opt-in complexity later.

Pilot highlights (what we observed):
- Pilot A (marketplace integration): most teams hit auth friction at step 2. Quick fix: provide a prefilled env file and CLI ...

Purpose:
This is a working canvas to map signal dependencies (metrics + traces) that feed our inference dashboards and SLI calculations. The focus is on framing which signals are direct vs derived, how dashboards should stitch traces to metrics, and where sampling or reduced fidelity is acceptable.

Context / problem statement:
- Observability sprawl: multiple dashboards show similar latency/quality stories but use different metric aggregations and trace anchors. Operators get different answers depending on which dashboard they consult.
- Root-cause gaps: when investigating latency spikes we often lack a clear hop-by-hop dependency map (model load, KV cache, routing, compilation). Traces exist but are not consistently linked to metricisers.

Goals for this canvas:
1) Produce a pragmatic signal dependency graph for the most-used dashboards (Ops, Rollouts, Cost).

Purpose and scope:
This is a working checklist + module notes for handling secrets eviction, graceful failover, and stateful Terraform upgrades during KMS/HSM integration and private deployment upgrades. Target audience: SREs and infra engineers running Redwood Private (VPC/on-prem) installs.

Why this exists (quick summary):

- Upgrade windows (Terraform module bumps, provider changes) need a safe, repeatable sequence that preserves key provenance and auditability.
- On-prem customers require deterministic audit chains and non-destructive rollback patterns for key material and operator actions.

title: Spectrum degrade strategy and control-plane scenarios
preview:
Purpose and scope:
This note captures a pragmatic design for graceful degradation across model fidelity, capacity tiers and regions. The goal is to define a control-plane-first approach that lets product traffic continue serving with predictable latency and cost characteristics while degrading user-visible quality i...
text:
Purpose and scope:
This note captures a pragmatic design for graceful degradation across model fidelity, capacity tiers and regions. The goal is to define a control-plane-first approach that lets product traffic continue serving with predictable latency and cost characteristics while degrading user-visible quality in controlled steps. Focus areas: routing policies, control-plane decision signals, multi-region spillover, and example policies/operators for rollout and emergency escape hatches.

Design motivation (short):
- Users need predictable behavior when capacity or latency SLOs break — not opaque errors.
- Instead of binary failover, model fidelity can be stepped down across a spectrum (quality, context length, quantization) to preserve availability.
- Control plane should make routing/variant decisions using compact signals (latency percentiles, queue length, effective concurrenc...

title: KV eviction signature analysis and host clock jitter effects
preview:
Purpose:
This doc captures a set of early experiments that attempt to correlate distinct eviction "signatures" (patterns in telemetry around which KV pages get evicted and when) with two under-explored sources of variability: host clock jitter across CPUs and short-lived I/O/bandwidth bursts that cause effective evi...
text:
Purpose:
This doc captures a set of early experiments that attempt to correlate distinct eviction "signatures" (patterns in telemetry around which KV pages get evicted and when) with two under-explored sources of variability: host clock jitter across CPUs and short-lived I/O/bandwidth bursts that cause effective eviction ordering changes. Goal is to decide whether we need: 1) cross-node clock-aligned watermarking for eviction, 2) jitter-aware eviction hysteresis, or 3) additional telemetry to reliably attribute long-tail misses to system-level noise rather than workload semantics.

Background / short primer:
- We track per-page last-access timestamp (logical monotonic ns) and use an LRU-ish eviction with hysteresis (min residency before eligible). This is sensitive to the clock used (rdtsc vs clock_gettime) and to inter-socket clock skew when sampling is done on different CPUs.
- Rece...

title: Onprem GPU Upgrade Sanity Playbook — Labbook
preview:
Purpose and scope:
This document is a working labbook-style playbook focused on safe, test-driven upgrades of NVIDIA drivers, CUDA, and NCCL across heterogeneous on-prem Kubernetes clusters that host Redwood Private. It complements the canonical upgrade playbooks (see linked Confluence) and surfaces field observatio...
text:
Purpose and scope:
This document is a working labbook-style playbook focused on safe, test-driven upgrades of NVIDIA drivers, CUDA, and NCCL across heterogeneous on-prem Kubernetes clusters that host Redwood Private. It complements the canonical upgrade playbooks (see linked Confluence) and surfaces field observations, validation checks, terraform module notes, and an automated sanity harness we use in the lab.

High-level upgrade strategy (fail-safe, rollback-first):
- Plan the rollout at node-pool granularity. Treat each node pool (by instance type + GPU family + kernel version) as a separate upgrade unit.
- Canary-first, rollback-first: apply the minimum change to a small canary pool and immediately run automated verification; if verification fails, trigger fast rollback automation.
- Keep a driver image snapshot and a node image snapshot (OS + kubelet + drivers) for instant reprov...

title: KV window coherence probes — long-context retrieval stress (8k/32k/128k)
preview:
Summary:
This note collects exploratory probes and regression-style tests focused on KV-cache window coherence under retrieval-heavy workloads across 8k, 32k and 128k context budgets. Goal is to exercise prefetch / sliding-window semantics, measure when prefix state decays (or is read incorrectly), and reproduce att...
text:
Summary:
This note collects exploratory probes and regression-style tests focused on KV-cache window coherence under retrieval-heavy workloads across 8k, 32k and 128k context budgets. Goal is to exercise prefetch / sliding-window semantics, measure when prefix state decays (or is read incorrectly), and reproduce attention-sink patterns when retrieval chunks interleave with model-generated prefixes.

Motivation / why: 
- We've seen intermittent hallucination/regression on models when recent retrieved chunks are reintroduced after forced truncation. Suspect a KV windowing coherence hole where older KV segments are partially present but stale.
